speaker: interviewer
text: --10th of September, 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia. Cameras are ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so how long were you involved in Habonim for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was involved with Habonim till about the middle 1949, when the Zionist movement became illegal and the whole organization got disbanded. And many of its leaders, organizers, and activists were jailed by the communist Hungarian government.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had they taken over in '49?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had taken over in '48. They had some things which was more urgent for them, before they started with the Zionists.

speaker: interviewer
text: And had Habonim been a very rewarding experience for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely. Definitely. There are friends from Habonim who are still friends. So it's a rewarding thing. It's-- it's just by chance that I finished up in Australia, and not in Israel where many of them did. A number of my friends were on the original And many of them live and work in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: The was a ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: The was an Aliya ship out of what Leon Uris made up the book and the film.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And was Habonim also something which helped you in terms of your experiences with the war, because you felt that you were doing something for the survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely. Definitely. Definitely. It was an experience. It was a growing up experience. It was leadership experience. It was thinking experience. I think it was more school than any school I went to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And after Habonim, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: After Habonim, I had to get lost into Hungary, because many of my close friends from Habonim with whom I worked got jailed. I was lucky that my Hebrew name is Yaakov. And out of that, it became Yaakie. And I was only known in the movement as Yaakie from Habonim, so the police didn't have much luck finding me. And to make sure that they don't, that is when I changed my name from Deutsch and wanted to go into the Army. I joined the Army as a volunteer in 1949, November, and served till 1953, January, leaving the Army as a regimental Sergeant Major.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, I was working for a ministry in different positions. While I was in the Army, I took a special HSC course, did my HSC. And when I started in the ministry, I started a correspondence evening university course.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you end up getting a degree in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I ended up a degree as economics and accountancy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in the ministry for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the ministry basically till I left Hungary, in different offices and groups of the ministry, back in the country in the last part in Budapest. And I left Budapest in 1956.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the revolution, there was an open border. And so, I got myself involved also in the revolution. And my sister left, because she wanted to. Earlier in November, the 23rd-- November the 23rd, she left Hungary. And she insisted that we should stay together. So in December-- end of December, I left Hungary and joined her in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from there, why-- and your mother as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother stayed. We had high-- look. Coming out from Hungary wasn't jumping on a bus. It was long walk. It was winter. It was risks. And it wasn't for my mother. And we had a very high hope that, when things settled down, elderly people, they will let out of Hungary. So we-- I joined to my sister in '57, January the 2nd, in Vienna. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you come decide on-- come to decide on Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the time I go to Vienna, my sister and the then husband-- they married in Vienna-- made up their mind that this is the furthest we can get from Europe, so this is where we're going. And I did. And I followed them in June the same year, arriving to Australia in July, 1957.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about that day of arrival in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Arriving in Australia, we came in a American troop carrier, through the Suez Canal, and arrived under the Harbour Bridge. And stepping off the ship, that was my sister. [EMOTIONAL]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a happy moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: And [NODS] that's how we started a new life here. They already prepared a place for me. Naturally, Peter-- Peter, the cousin-- was with me. We came out together. We stayed together, and we came out here together. And they already prepared place for us. And we lived together to start the life up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kinds of jobs did you do, to start off with?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first job was at the railway, at Redford, at the Eveleigh locomotive shop, loading wagons and helping with the repairing of the wheels of the locomotives. I-- I want to-- [EMOTIONAL] mention here too, it's Rosh Hashanah again. And in that not-English-speaking group of workers, there was two of us Hungarian Jews. [BLOWS NOSE] And we decided that we will celebrate Rosh Hashanah and not to go to work, even so that money was very, very scarce. And to our biggest surprise, at the end of the week, when we got our wages envelope, we got the full wages with a card in it [CRYING] wishing us a Happy New Year from the railways. [SOBS] It was absolute uncounted on [INAUDIBLE]. And then, life slowly went on. We made new friends. [BLOWS NOSE] We tried to learn English and settle in the new country. I left the railways and worked for a chocolate factory as a driver, which was a big step up. And then, in '58, I got a job with a firm called, [? XSO Craft, ?] as a driver. I started working there early in September. In the meanwhile, my mother and her sister, [? Berge, ?] Peter's mother, were able to leave Hungary. And because we couldn't give her guarantees here, so they were able to go out to London to some relatives, till we worked out the things here. And here, again, with the help of the joint committee at [NON-ENGLISH], we were able to furnish guarantees and ship tickets. So they were coming after us, in September, in '58.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about her arrival?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her arrival is two things what stuck in the memory. I told the boss that, next day, I can't come to work, because my mother arrives. My English was nearly nonexistent, so with hand and foot I explained it. And he made a very stern face. [CRYING] Excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just take--

speaker: interviewee
text: And told me that I have to come in next morning, before I go to pick up my mother, because I have to take his car, that I can get my mother and take my mother home with with a car. [CRYING] [BLOWS NOSE] Arriving to Woolloomooloo, there my mother's ship pulls in. Somehow, I don't know how, we were able to get on the ship to greet her there. And she was very happy to see us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the first thing was that, darling, what shall I do with that? How do I do with that? She was able to smuggle out from Hungary, my father gold cigarette case. And she was worried how she brings it through [CRYING] the Australian customs. And that was her biggest worry. [SOBS] And you were able to tell her that--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I explained that she can give it to me. Nobody will worry about that sort of things here. Then, I settled down with my mother. We lived together. She was working. I was working. And then, in '61, July, I got married. And still living happily ever after. [EMOTIONAL] I have two children, a son and daughter. [? XSO ?] [? Craft ?] is still in our life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I represent the firm, in the last 30 years. And that is the story about.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'll just ask you some general questions now. It sounds like you feel that Australia is definitely your home. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- how do you feel about Australia and Australians? It sounds like it was a lot of hard work for you. But at the same time, you did get the support.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look. I don't think that, in any other place in the world, with the things what I started with, communist economics, the one language of Hungarian, I could have do as well as I think I did-- or we did, because my wife plays a big part of it, to put us where we are, to bring up the children, as I think we did. [EMOTIONAL] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the bigger half of our life, we spent here. And we just have thanks to-- to say to this country. Accepted us and even changed its ways to more our ways. I remember very well that, when we came out here, to get a piece of continental bread, you had to order it a week ahead, because there was one bakery, [? Kritzler, ?] who did it in Mumbai Junction. And today, you go into the smallest [NON-ENGLISH], and you can get as many different bread as you can imagine. And even that they don't profit off it, they still like chabi and Hungarian salami. [LAUGHS] And [INAUDIBLE]. And they even visit our club [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were saying before that, after the war with the Zionist movement, for a while you wavering between believing in the whole Jewish faith and not. What ended up happening? How do you feel about the Jewish faith now?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are not orthodox. We haven't got a Kosher home, but we are Jewish. We are members of a Lubavitch synagogue. In the last two years, three years, I became deputy president of the [NON-ENGLISH] synagogue. And my wife is active in the lady's auxiliary. I'm a member of the Jewish board of deputies of [NON-ENGLISH]. And we try to help as many organizations as we can. We visit Israel. We help Israel. We work for Israel. We are very active in the United Israel Appeal. And we hope that our children will carry on the tradition. My son is married. He's married to a very nice Jewish girl, also of Hungarian-Israeli descent. [EMOTIONAL] And we hope they will bring up the grandchildren-- what's coming-- [LAUGHS] on the same way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were also saying before that, when you left Hungary, you felt like you didn't know who's hand you were shaking anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about Hungary? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Look. I visited Hungary since. I want to visit Hungary. I still have [BLOWS NOSE] some young age memories there. I have some family there who are very dear from us. I still have some friends there. [EMOTIONAL] But I get very upset when people talk about-- people, and the people I mean, my friends, who went through the same things-- talk about Hungary, that it's home. It's not home. [SOBS] It's never been that. [CRIES]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And if you could give a message to the Nazis and the Arrow Cross members, what would you say to them now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have nothing to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about to the people watching this video? Do you have any messages that you would give them from your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look. The message is-- is clearly written. We should do something about it, that it cannot happen again. But unfortunately, it's happening, non-stop. [EMOTIONAL] In Yugoslavia, [INAUDIBLE] people just don't want to live in peace or let people live in peace. And unfortunately, it goes on in Israel too. But there are hopes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Devai, thank you very much. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Ivan, would you like to introduce everybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Introduce-- to start with my darling wife, that's Judy. My older daughter, Annette. My son, Robert, and my new daughter, daughter-in-law, Desi.

speaker: interviewer
text: How new?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only two years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost three.

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost three? [LAUGHTER] Time flies. Time flies.

speaker: interviewee
text: In November.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So, would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Go for it, Mum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spokesperson. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I don't know where-- where to start.

speaker: interviewee
text: Beginning, darling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- [LAUGHS] that's too long. [LAUGHTER] I'm also a survivor, except I was too young to remember. I was around three years of age, so my memory's really very-- well, I've got no memory of the war years. I suppose, even after the war years, I was sheltered, because I didn't want to think too much about the war years. But I-- over the years, I-- I know we-- we suffered. I-- I was hiding with my mother under false papers. And but I'm blessed now with a beautiful family. And-- [LAUGHTER] And I suppose the one mention I should say, that, after the war I was-- my mother had a-- a son, who was named Ivan. And I just happened to find the right husband, whose name is Ivan. And only after we named our son, I realized that my young brother's second name was Robert. And now, our family's prospering. We've got a beautiful younger daughter. And we're very happy. And we're very close, and the whole family. We always have been a very close family, as a child as well. And here we are, blessed that some of our family members who went through a lot during the war and survived, they're here in Australia, as well, with us. And we're fortunate that we are in this beautiful country. And I always maintain that I just happened to be born in Hungary, but I have no special fond or happy memories of Hungary. This is home, and this is where my happiness is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, very much. Now, the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're the child.

speaker: interviewer
text: The three children?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's the child.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's the child. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: She's the-- she's younger than me. Come on. Let's hear something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I haven't been a Devai for that long, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: All I can say is that, if it wasn't for Dad, we wouldn't be here. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, And Mum.

speaker: interviewer
text: True.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I love my Daddy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He's not bad. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Anything else you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you couldn't ask for nicer parents-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: Aw.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's true. [INTERPOSING VOICES] [LAUGHTER] It's true.

speaker: interviewee
text: Someone's looking for brownie points. [LAUGHTER] I'm getting [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait till later.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Thank you, very much. That's great.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, can you tell me about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That is my grandfather's shop. [NON-ENGLISH] Fulop. And that photo is from about 1911. The two people is my grandfather and my grandmother. And between them, the older girl is my mother. And the smaller girl is my mother sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Budapest at [NON-ENGLISH]. In their own house, they had this big delicatessen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my father in 1917, somewhere in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Oh, and-- and your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father name is Deutsch, Ferenc.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- and so that's Ferenc Deutsch?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Ferenc Deutsch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture is 1930, and it's my mother and father on their honeymoon at St. Mark's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Square.

speaker: interviewee
text: Square.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Venice.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Venice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name is Janka Appel. And here, she became Janka Deutsch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is in 1940. And that is my uncle, my mother's brother, Arpad, and the wife, Roszi-- Roszi, who was with us in-- all along in this yellow star house, and et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was taken in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Budapest, 1940, somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who is this here?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my grandmother. And I'm sitting on the window sill. The picture was taken in Budapest, in 1932.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this is your mother's mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my mother's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Roza Appel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these children?

speaker: interviewee
text: These children are-- the younger boy is Peter, the cousin. The girl in the middle is my sister, Marica. And other one is me. The picture is somewhere in 1942, '43, in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your cousin's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Klein. Peter Klein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my sister with the doll, Chippie. That was one of her favorites.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does Chippie mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bitsy. Little. Little.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken also in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest, at the kindergarten.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This one is the last photo of my father. It is done in the backyard in our house, somewhere '42, '43, in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go. That is a picture of me, also 1942, Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then? 10.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that is one of the first photos after the war. That is 1947. I just started technical college.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Last photo from Budapest. 1956, Christmas.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before leaving Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me, with the pipe.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is the first family gathering in Sydney. On the left, that's my sister, who is waving. Next to it, the husband. Then Peter and me on the background. And in the front is Marica's husband's uncle, auntie, cousin, and the little cousin in the front. That is [NON-ENGLISH], '94, Randwick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewer
text: That is our engagement photo, 1960, December. And that's you and your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me and my wife. For a change, she looks dark, but she was blonde.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name is Judy-- Judy and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a quite recent photo of my mother. The photo has been taken with my-- by my very artistic daughter, Annette. And my mother, [NON-ENGLISH], is 90 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Still with us, here in Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she lives on her own?

speaker: interviewee
text: She lives on her own, looks after herself. And still keeps herself very busy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the only piece of family heirloom left. That candelabras belonged to my father's parents. Went through. My mother had it. Now, I have it, and I will leave it to my children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know how they were salvaged?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. By miracles. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still use them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape five of the interview with Ivan Devai. It's the 17th of September, 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia. And that's also the end of the whole interview.
